title: Reducing single-use plastic in the bathroom
subtitle: Bar soap, shampoo bars, bamboo toothbrushes, and refill systems: a lifecycle comparison
excerpt: The bathroom produces a large share of household single-use plastic: shampoo bottles, conditioner, liquid soap, shower gel, razors, toothbrushes, and toothpaste tubes. Simple swaps such as bar soap and a safety razor cut plastic waste. Each swap has real trade-offs in cost and convenience.
sourceNotes: WRAP UK Plastics Pact data on bathroom product plastic consumption. Which? green living guide to bathroom alternatives (2023). Mintel UK personal care market data for refillable format growth. Life cycle assessment data from University of Sheffield (bar soap vs liquid soap LCA, 2019). Zero Waste Scotland bathroom product packaging audit.

content.text: A typical UK household discards 8-12 plastic personal care bottles per person per year: shampoo, conditioner, shower gel, liquid soap, face wash. At 250-500 ml each, that is 3-6 litres of plastic per person annually. Most is technically recyclable but in practice inconsistently collected and processed. The most effective reductions are in high-volume categories: shampoo and body wash.

A 2019 lifecycle assessment from the University of Sheffield found that bar soap has around 25% lower carbon footprint than liquid soap per wash (partly from the product itself, partly from the avoided plastic packaging). Bar soap is 2-8 times more concentrated than liquid soap: a 100 g bar replaces approximately 300-400 ml of liquid soap. The main barrier is habitual preference. Bar soap performs identically for hand-washing; quality cold-process bars (palm-free, unscented) are effective for body washing.

A shampoo bar typically replaces 2-3 bottles of liquid shampoo. Most use surfactants (sodium cocoyl isethionate, sodium lauryl sulfoacetate) that cleanse without silicones. The main adjustment required: most people experience a 1-3 week transition period where hair feels waxy or heavy as the scalp adjusts to the change in pH and surfactant type. Using a dilute apple cider vinegar rinse (50 ml in 500 ml water) after washing reduces this transition period. Hard water (above 250 mg/l of calcium carbonate, which is most of south-east England) can cause more mineral build-up with shampoo bars than with liquid products.

A safety razor (double-edged, with replaceable blades) has zero plastic waste beyond the packaging for spare blades. A spare blade costs 5-30p (sold in packs of 5-100); a disposable cartridge razor replacement costs a modest cost. For anyone who shaves regularly, the switch to a safety razor saves a modest cost and eliminates all plastic from that routine. The learning curve is a few weeks to master pressure control: a safety razor requires less pressure than a cartridge razor.

Several UK brands (Faith in Nature, Bower Collective, Splosh) offer concentrated refill tablets or pouches that dissolve in a reusable bottle. The bottle is bought once; refills are sent by post in lightweight packaging. Refills typically cost 30-40% less than equivalent retail product volume. The effective plastic reduction is 80-90% compared to buying individual bottles each time.
What not to switch without trialling

Toothpaste tablets: work for most people but some find them less effective at plaque removal than conventional paste. Rinse thoroughly. Bamboo toothbrushes: the nylon bristles are not biodegradable; remove and bin them before composting the handle. The handle itself makes up less than 20% of toothbrush weight; the bigger gain is in the packaging rather than the brush material. Menstrual cup or reusable period products: not a packaging question but eliminates a significant waste stream; worth researching separately if relevant.
